subject: Chemistry
q: Consider a mixture ' X ' which is made by dissolving 0.4 mol of $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right] \mathrm{Br}$ and 0.4 mol of $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Br}\right] \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ in water to make 4 L of solution. When 2 L of mixture ' X ' is allowed to react with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$, it forms precipitate ' Y '. The rest 2 L of mixture ' X ' reacts with excess $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ to form precipitate ' Z '. Which of the following statements is CORRECT?
question: Consider a mixture ' X ' which is made by dissolving 0.4 mol of $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right] \mathrm{Br}$ and 0.4 mol of $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Br}\right] \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ in water to make 4 L of solution. When 2 L of mixture ' X ' is allowed to react with excess of $\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}$, it forms precipitate ' Y '. The rest 2 L of mixture ' X ' reacts with excess $\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}$ to form precipitate ' Z '. Which of the following statements is CORRECT?
solution:
0.4 mol $[Co(NH_3)_5SO_4]Br$ + 0.4 mol $[Co(NH_3)_5Br]SO_4$ are present in 4 lit. solution.
2 lit. of mixture will contain 0.2 mol of each complex.
2 lit. mixture on reaction with excess $AgNO_3$, 0.2 mole $AgBr$ will be formed (Y).
2 lit. mixture on reaction with excess $BaCl_2$, 0.2 mole $BaSO_4$ will be formed (Z).

subject: Chemistry
q: The homoleptic and octahedral complex of Co2+ and H2O has ______ unpaired electron(s) in the t2g set of orbitals.
question: The homoleptic and octahedral complex of Co2+ and H2O has ______ unpaired electron(s) in the t2g set of orbitals.
solution:
The electronic configuration of Co2+ is 3d7. In an octahedral complex of Co2+ with H2O ligands, the d orbitals split into two sets of energy levels: t2g (lower energy) and eg (higher energy).

In this case, since the complex is octahedral, we have six ligands surrounding the central cobalt ion. Each ligand donates a pair of electrons to the cobalt ion, occupying the available d orbitals.

So, CoH2O62+=d7High spin=t2g5 eg2

In the t2g set of orbitals, there will be 1 unpaired electron

q: Which of the following complex has a possibility to exist as meridional isomer?

solution:
The type of the complex MA3B3 exhibit the geometrical isomerism fac-mer isomerism. When three ligands and the metal are in one plane, the isomer is said to be meridional, or mer.

[Co(NH3)3(NO2)3] can show facial and meridional isomerism.

The number of chloride ions satisfying the secondary valency of the metal ion is ______ .
question: Three moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>AgCl</mi></math> get precipitated when one mole of an octahedral co-ordination compound with empirical formula <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CrCl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>.</mo><mn>3</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> reacts with excess of silver nitrate. The number of chloride ions satisfying the secondary valency of the metal ion is ______ .
solution:
Mole of AgCl precipitated is equal the mole of Cl- present in ionization sphere.

Since none of Cl- is present in the co ordination sphere. Therefore answer is zero.

q: The pair in which both the species have the same magnetic moment (spin only) is:
question: The pair in which both the species have the same magnetic moment (spin only) is:
solution:
No. unpaired e-
CrH2O62+⇒Cr2+⇒3d4⇒t2g1,1,1, eg1,0 4
FeH2O62+⇒Fe2+⇒3d6⇒t2g1,1,1, eg1,1 4
FeNH362+⇒Fe2+⇒3d6⇒t2g2,1,1, eg1,1 4
CoOH42-⇒Co2+⇒3d7⇒ eg2,2, t21,1,1 3
CoCI42-⇒Co2+⇒3d7⇒ e2,2, t21,1,1 3
MnH2O62+⇒MN2+⇒3d5⇒ t2g1,1,1, eg1,1 5
So CrH2O62+ and FeH2O62+  have same magnetic moment (spin only)

solution:
Ligands which have two different donor sites but at a time connects with only one donor site to central metal are ambidentate ligands.
Ambidentate ligands are $\mathrm{NO}_2^{-} ; \mathrm{SCN}^{-} ; \mathrm{CN}^{-}$

solution:
In facial isomer, three Cl atoms present on one face of the octahedron. Similarly, three ammonia molecules are present on one face of the octahedron. All 3 same ligands have 90° bond angle between them.

The Cl-Co-Cl bond angle in above octahedral complex is 90°

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following complex ions has electrons that are symmetrically filled in both <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></msub></math> orbitals?
question: Which of the following complex ions has electrons that are symmetrically filled in both <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></msub></math> orbitals?
options: <math><msup><mrow><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><msub><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math>
solution:
Metal ion having either d5 or d10 configuration can be filled symmetrically in both t2g and eg orbitals. Co+2 has d7 configuration, Co+3 has d6 configuration. Mn+2 has d5 configuration but CN- is strong field ligand and it will pair the electrons due to which electronic configuration is t2g5eg0.

As F is weak field ligand hence back pairing of electrons do not take place or in case of weak field ligand pairing energy of electrons (P )is greater than crystal field splitting energy in octahedral complex ∆o i.e.P>∆o , hence electrons will go in eg orbitals, instead of back paired in t2g orbitals.

q: A transition metal \((\mathrm{M})\) among \(\mathrm{Mn}, \mathrm{Cr}, \mathrm{Co}\) and Fe has the highest standard electrode potential \(\left(\mathrm{M}^{3+} / \mathrm{M}^{2+}\right)\). It forms a metal complex of the type \(\left[\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}\). The number of electrons present in the \(\mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}\) orbital of the complex is _________.
question: A transition metal \((\mathrm{M})\) among \(\mathrm{Mn}, \mathrm{Cr}, \mathrm{Co}\) and Fe has the highest standard electrode potential \(\left(\mathrm{M}^{3+} / \mathrm{M}^{2+}\right)\). It forms a metal complex of the type \(\left[\mathrm{M}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}\). The number of electrons present in the \(\mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}\) orbital of the complex is _________.
solution:
Co has highest standard electrode potential \(\left(\mathrm{M}^{+3} / \mathrm{M}^{+2}\right)\) among \(\mathrm{Mn}, \mathrm{Cr}, \mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Fe}\)
\(\therefore\) Complex is \(\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}\) and its splitting is as follows.

\(\therefore\) electron in \(\mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}\) orbital is one.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following cannot be explained by crystal field theory?
question: Which of the following cannot be explained by crystal field theory?
options: Magnetic properties of transition metal complexes
solution: The crystal field model is successful in explaining the formation, structures, colour and magnetic properties of coordination compounds to a large extent. However, from the assumptions that the ligands are point charges, it follows that anionic ligands should exert the greatest splitting effect. The anionic ligands actually are found at the low end of the spectrochemical series. So, it couldn't explain successfully the order of spectrochemical series.

q: The magnetic moment is measured in Bohr Magneton (BM). Spin only magnetic moment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Fe</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Fe</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Fe</mi><msub><mfenced><mi>CN</mi></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> complexes respectively is:
question: The magnetic moment is measured in Bohr Magneton (BM). Spin only magnetic moment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Fe</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Fe</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Fe</mi><msub><mfenced><mi>CN</mi></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> complexes respectively is:
solution:
The spin only magnetic moment can be calculated as follows

μ=nn+2BM

n=Number of unpaired electrons

FeH2O63+=d5(High spin complex)=5.unpaired electrons
μ=5×7=35
=5.92 B.M.
FeCN63+=d5(Low spin complex)=1
μ=1×3=3
=1.732 B.M.

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Co</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><msub><mi>CoF</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> are respectively known as:
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Co</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><msub><mi>CoF</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> are respectively known as:
options: Spin free Complex, Spin paired Complex
Spin paired Complex, Spin free Complex
Outer orbital Complex, Inner orbital Complex
Inner orbital Complex, Spin paired Complex
solution:
In the complex CoNH363+ Ammonia is strong field ligand and electronic configuration of 

Co3+⇒3 d6t2 g6,eg0

Hybridisation: d2sp3

Pairing of electrons takes place due to the strong field ligand. Hence, it is inner orbital complex (spin paired complex).

In the complex CoF63- fluoride is weak field ligand and electronic configuration of  Co3+ ⇒3d6t2g4,eg2.

Hybridisation: sp3d2

Pairing of electrons not takes place due to the weak field ligand. Hence, it is outer orbital complex (spin free complex).

Hence option B is the answer.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The statements that are incorrect about the nickel(II) complex of dimethylglyoxime are :
A. It is red in colour.
B. It has a high solubility in water at $\mathrm{pH}=9$.
C. The Ni ion has two unpaired d-electrons.
D. The $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{Ni}-\mathrm{N}$ bond angle is almost close to $90^{\circ}$.
E. The complex contains four five-membered metallacycles (metal containing rings).
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
The statements that are incorrect about the nickel(II) complex of dimethylglyoxime are :
A. It is red in colour.
B. It has a high solubility in water at $\mathrm{pH}=9$.
C. The Ni ion has two unpaired d-electrons.
D. The $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{Ni}-\mathrm{N}$ bond angle is almost close to $90^{\circ}$.
E. The complex contains four five-membered metallacycles (metal containing rings).
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Statement A is incorrect as the complex is yellow-green, not red.
Statement B is incorrect because the complex shows low solubility in water, especially at neutral pH.
Statement C is incorrect because Ni(II) in square planar geometry has all d-electrons paired (d^8 configuration), not unpaired electrons.
Statement D is correct regarding the N-Ni-N bond angle being approximately 90° in square planar geometry.
Statement E is correct as the complex contains two five-membered metallacycles (chelate rings) per dimethylglyoxime molecule.
The incorrect statements are B, C, and E.

q:
Match List I with List II

  LIST – I
(Complex ion)		   LIST – II
(Electronic
Configuration
A. CrH2O63+ I. t2g2eg0
B. FeH2O63+ II. t2g3eg0
C. NiH2O62+ III. t2g3eg2
D. VH2O63+ IV. t2g6eg2

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Match List I with List II

  LIST – I
(Complex ion)		   LIST – II
(Electronic
Configuration
A. CrH2O63+ I. t2g2eg0
B. FeH2O63+ II. t2g3eg0
C. NiH2O62+ III. t2g3eg2
D. VH2O63+ IV. t2g6eg2

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
The t2g (triplet, lower energy) orbitals are the dxy, dxz and dyz orbitals. The eg (doublet, higher energy) orbitals are the dz2 and dx2-y2 orbitals. These split d-orbitals are called t2g and egbecause they are split by the effect of the surrounding ligand ions in a crystal field.

CrH2O63+ Contains Cr3+:Ar3d3:t2g3ego
FeH2O63+ Contains Fe3+:Ar3d5:t2g3eg2
NiH2O62+ Contains Ni2+:Ar3d8:t2g6eg2

VH2O63+ Contains V3+:Ar3d2:t2g2ego

subject: Chemistry
q: The total number of stereoisomers for the complex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mi>Cr</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>ox</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>ClBr</mi><msup><mo>]</mo><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>–</mo></mrow></msup></math> (where ox = oxalate) is
question: The total number of stereoisomers for the complex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>[</mo><mi>Cr</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>ox</mi><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msub><mi>ClBr</mi><msup><mo>]</mo><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>–</mo></mrow></msup></math> (where ox = oxalate) is
solution: The complex [Cr(ox)2ClBr]3– exhibits two geometrical isomers. Among these isomers Cis-isomer is optically active.  Hence, the total number of stereoisomers for the given complex is 3.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is correct order of ligand field strength?
question: Which of the following is correct order of ligand field strength?
solution:
According to spectrochemical series, ligands can be arranged in a series in the order of increasing field strength as given below: 

I –  < Br–  < SCN –  < Cl –  < S2–  < F –  < OH –  < C2O4 2–  < H2O < NCS – < edta4–  < NH3  < en < CN –  < CO

As per the above series, correct answer is S2-<C2O42-<NH3<en<CO

subject: Chemistry
q: From the magnetic behaviour of $\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}$ (paramagnetic) and $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4\right]$ (diamagnetic), choose the correct geometry and oxidation state.
question: From the magnetic behaviour of $\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}$ (paramagnetic) and $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4\right]$ (diamagnetic), choose the correct geometry and oxidation state.
solution:
$\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}$
$\mathrm{Ni}^{+2}-[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^8 4 \mathrm{~s}^0 \rightarrow \mathrm{sp}^3$, Tetrahedral
Number of unpaired electron $=2$ paramagentic
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4\right]$
$\mathrm{Ni}(0) \rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^{10} 4 \mathrm{~s}^0$ (After rearrangement)
No unpaired electron
$\mathrm{sp}^3$, Tetrahedral, Diamagnetic

subject: Chemistry
q: The oxidation states of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Cr</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi>CN</mi></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced><mfenced separators="|"><msub><mi>NH</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msub></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math>, respectively, are:
question: The oxidation states of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Cr</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>6</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi>CN</mi></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced><mfenced separators="|"><msub><mi>NH</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo></mrow></msub></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math>, respectively, are:
solution:
(i)   CrH2O6Cl3: Hexaaquachromium III chloride

x+6×0+-1×3=0

x=+3

(ii)  CrC6H62:bis η6-benzenechromium 0

y+2×0=0

y=0

(iii)   K2CrCN2O2O2NH3: Potassium amminedicyanido dioxide peroxide chromate VI

2×1+z+2×-1+2×-2+-2+0=0

z=+6

The oxidation states of Cr in CrH2O6Cl3, CrC6H62 and K2CrCN2O2O2NH3, respectively, are +3, 0 and +6.

subject: Chemistry
q: Low oxidation state of metals in their complexes are common when ligands
question: Low oxidation state of metals in their complexes are common when ligands
solution:
When metal has low oxidation state, it has more electron density in d orbital. So it has more tendency to back donate electrons. Thus compound must have good π acceptor ligand.

 π backbonding, also called π backdonation, is when electrons move from an atomic orbital on one atom to an appropriate symmetry antibonding orbital on a π-acceptor ligand.It is especially common in the organometallic chemistry of transition metals with multi-atomic ligands such as carbon monoxide, ethylene or the nitrosonium cation. Electrons from the metal are used to bond to the ligand, in the process relieving the metal of excess negative charge. Compounds where π backbonding occurs include Ni(CO)4 and Zeise's salt.

The metal to ligand bonding creates a synergic effect which strengthens the bond between ligand and the metal

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements, one is labelled as assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.
assertion A : CoClNH352+ absorbs at lower wavelength of light with respect to CoNH35H2O3+
Reason R: It is because the wavelenght of light absorbed depends on the oxidation state of the metal ion.
In the light of the above statements,choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements, one is labelled as assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R.
assertion A : CoClNH352+ absorbs at lower wavelength of light with respect to CoNH35H2O3+
Reason R: It is because the wavelenght of light absorbed depends on the oxidation state of the metal ion.
In the light of the above statements,choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> is false but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is true
<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> is true but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is false

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> are true and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is the correct explaination of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math>

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> are true and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">R</mi></math> is NOT the correct explaination of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="bold">A</mi></math>
solution: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>CoCl</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>5</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math>absorbs with higher wavelength of light with respect to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Co</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>5</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> because <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Cl</mi><mo>-</mo></msup></math> is weaker ligand than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>. The crystal field stabilisation is higher for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Co</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>5</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math>, hence we can say that wavelength of light absorbed depends upon the oxidation state of the metal ion.

subject: Chemistry
q: Two complexes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>6</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><msub><mfenced separators="|"><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mfenced></math>  are violet and yellow coloured, respectively, The incorrect statement regarding them is:
question: Two complexes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>6</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="[" separators="|"><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><msub><mfenced separators="|"><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mfenced></math>  are violet and yellow coloured, respectively, The incorrect statement regarding them is:
options: Both are paramagnetic with three unpaired electrons

<math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math>  value of  <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>  is less than that of  <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>

<math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> values of <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> are calculated from the energies violet and yellow light, respectively.
Both absorb energies corresponding to their complementary colors.
solution:
∆0A<∆0B , both complex have CN=6, B has strong field ligand and A has weak field ligand

so ∆0A< ∆0B

In both complexes, Cr has 3 unpaired electrons and hence, will be paramagnetic CrH2OCl3

Cr+3=1s2  2s2  2p6 3s2 3p6  3d3 in strong field ligand or weak field ligand is t2g1,1,1,ego



Δ0 order will be compared by spectrochemical series not by energies of violet and yellow light. Hence incorrect

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following complex ions will exhibit optical isomerism? (en = 1, 2-diamine ethane).
question: Which of the following complex ions will exhibit optical isomerism? (en = 1, 2-diamine ethane).
solution: Non superimposable mirror images are called optical isomers and may be described as "chiral'. They are also called enantiomers and rotate plane polarised light in opposite directions.

subject: Chemistry
q: The number of optical isomers possible for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> is:
question: The number of optical isomers possible for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> is:
solution: The number of optical isomers for CrC2O433-is two (d-isomer and l-isomer).

subject: Chemistry
q: The d-orbital electronic configuration of the complex among $\quad\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{en})_3\right]^{3+}, \quad\left[\mathrm{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}$, $\left[\mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$ that has the highest CFSE is :
question: The d-orbital electronic configuration of the complex among $\quad\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{en})_3\right]^{3+}, \quad\left[\mathrm{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}$, $\left[\mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Zn}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$ that has the highest CFSE is :
solution:
In $\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{en})_3\right]^{+3}$ S.F.L. is present and hence highest value of CFSE
In rest all complexes WFL is present hence CFSE will be low.
$\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{en})_3\right]^{+3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{Co}^{+3}$ and en (SFL)
$\mathrm{Co}^{+3} \Rightarrow[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 \mathrm{~d}^6$

subject: Chemistry
q: A reaction of cobalt <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>III</mi><mo>)</mo></math> chloride and ethylenediamine in a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></math> mole ratio generates two isomeric products <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> (violet-coloured) and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> (green-coloured). <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> can show optical activity, but, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is optically inactive. What type of isomers do <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> represent?
question: A reaction of cobalt <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>III</mi><mo>)</mo></math> chloride and ethylenediamine in a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>2</mn></math> mole ratio generates two isomeric products <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> (violet-coloured) and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> (green-coloured). <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> can show optical activity, but, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> is optically inactive. What type of isomers do <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> represent?
solution:
The coordination number of cobalt is 6.

The mole ratio of CoCl3 and en is 1 : 2.

Hence,

CoCl3+2 en→Coen2Cl2 Cl

The complex formed shows geometrical isomerism.

The complex exists as cis and trans isomers among which the trans isomer is optically inactive.

subject: Chemistry
q: On treatment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CoCl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> with excess <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AgNO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>;</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.2</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>22</mn></msup></math> ions are precipitated. The complex is:
question: On treatment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CoCl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> with excess <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>AgNO</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>;</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.2</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>22</mn></msup></math> ions are precipitated. The complex is:
solution:
Moles of CoCl3.6H2O=100×0.1×10-3=10-2mole

Moles of Cl- ions precipitated=1.2×10226×1023=2×10-2 mole

So, one mole of complex will release two moles of chloride ions so, formula of complex is 

CoH2O5ClCl2. H2O
